Migrating data to an object store (the Arbutus recipe)

Source: vignettes/migration-workflow.Rmd
migration-workflow.Rmd

This is the end-to-end recipe used to move large GeoTIFF datasets from Google Drive to the Digital Research Alliance of Canada’s Arbutus object storage, expose them via /vsicurl/ for remote-readable access in R (terra), and provide a browsable HTML catalogue with buckethost.

It is deliberately specific — future-you debugging a six-month-later problem will want the exact flags and gotchas, not a sanitised summary. Most of it is dashboard / shell work done once; the R parts are at the end.

Why object storage over Google Drive?

  • Google Drive doesn’t support HTTP range requests reliably, so partial reads with terra::rast("/vsicurl/...") don’t work — every read pulls the whole file.
  • Arbutus is S3/Swift-compatible, Canadian-hosted, and free for academic PIs (Rapid Access Service allocation).
  • Once files are Cloud-Optimized GeoTIFFs (COGs) on Arbutus, terra::rast() + crop() transfers only the bytes needed for the area of interest — typically a few hundred KB per query.

Why a fast transfer node, not your usual login host?

A government-network host gave ~1 MB/s sustained during testing; a 321 GB migration would have taken days. The Alliance’s Fir cluster is on the CANARIE research network with ~600+ MB/s to both Drive and Arbutus — the same 321 GB completes in minutes. Run the transfer there.

Phase 0 — accounts and credentials (one-time, dashboard)

A. CCDB account active. At https://ccdb.alliancecan.ca: confirm PI status and active allocations; under My Account → Access Systems enable the cluster you’ll transfer from (e.g. Fir); under Multifactor Authentication Management enroll a Duo device and save 10 backup codes.

B. Arbutus cloud project + storage. Request via the RAS (Rapid Access Service) form on CCDB. The form requires non-zero VM resources even for object-storage-only use, so choose the minimum (1 vCPU, 1 instance, 1 volume, 1.5 GB RAM, 1 floating IP, 20 GB persistent). The number that matters is Object Storage — request what you need. Justify: hosting COG outputs for collaborator access via /vsicurl/; HPC clusters don’t fit (they’re for compute, not public HTTPS serving); include a maintenance plan. Turnaround is typically a week or two.

C. EC2 (S3-compatible) credentials. The OpenStack Application Credentials in the dashboard are not what you want — those use Swift-native auth, not S3. The Horizon dashboard removed the direct “EC2 Credentials” button, so use the CLI:

pipx install python-openstackclient        # or pip install --user on Windows
source ~/Downloads/<project>-openrc.sh
openstack ec2 credentials create

This needs port 5000 (Keystone), which is blocked on many government networks. Workaround: run it once from a home network or cellular hotspot. The resulting EC2 access key + secret are portable — once created they work from anywhere over port 443. Save them to a password manager.

D. Public container. In the Arbutus dashboard: Project → Object Store → Containers → Create container named e.g. predictiveecology. Set its access to Public so /vsicurl/ reads work without authentication.

Phase 1 — configure rclone on the transfer node

Fir ships rclone (verify with rclone version). You need two remotes: arbutus (S3) and gdrive (Google Drive source).

1a. The arbutus S3 remote

rclone config → n (new) → name arbutus → storage s3 → provider Other → env_auth false → paste your EC2 access key / secret → region blank → endpoint https://object-arbutus.cloud.computecanada.ca → location_constraint blank → acl private → Edit advanced config: yes, and set the one critical option:

force_path_style = true

Arbutus’s Swift S3 gateway doesn’t support virtual-hosted-style URLs (bucket.endpoint.com). Without force_path_style = true, every request fails with signature or DNS errors. Accept defaults for everything else. The resulting ~/.config/rclone/rclone.conf should contain:

[arbutus]
type = s3
provider = Other
access_key_id = <key>
secret_access_key = <secret>
endpoint = https://object-arbutus.cloud.computecanada.ca
acl = private
force_path_style = true

Test: rclone lsd arbutus: should list your container(s) with no error.

1b. The gdrive remote

Google Drive OAuth needs a browser, but the node is headless. Bridge the callback with SSH port forwarding from your laptop:

ssh -L 53682:localhost:53682 <user>@fir.alliancecan.ca

In that same session: rclone config → n → name gdrive → storage drive → client_id/secret blank → scope drive.readonly (safer for a one-way migration) → service_account_file blank → advanced config n → “Use web browser to automatically authenticate?” y. rclone prints a http://127.0.0.1:53682/auth?state=... URL — paste it into your laptop browser; the SSH tunnel forwards it to rclone waiting on Fir. Authorize, then “Configure as Shared Drive?” n (unless it’s a Team Drive) → confirm.

Test: rclone lsd gdrive: lists your Drive folders. If your data is under “Shared with me”, either add a My Drive shortcut to it, or add --drive-shared-with-me to commands targeting that path.

Phase 2 — migrate

Work inside tmux so the transfer survives disconnects: tmux new -s migrate (detach with Ctrl-b d, reattach with tmux attach -t migrate).

2a. Filter file (explicit top-to-bottom ordering, first match wins):

cat > ~/scanfi.filter << 'EOF'
- *.tif.tiff
+ *.tif
+ *.tif.ovr
+ *.tif.aux.xml
- *
EOF

2b. Run the copy, looping over subfolders. Note the destination uses underscores (SCANFI_v2) instead of the source’s space for cleaner URLs:

for year in 1985 1990 1995 2000 2005 2010 2015 2020 2025; do
  echo "=== $(date) Starting $year ==="
  rclone copy \
    "gdrive:Shared/Hosted/SCANFI/SCANFI v2/$year" \
    "arbutus:predictiveecology/SCANFI_v2/$year" \
    --filter-from ~/scanfi.filter \
    --transfers 16 --timeout 5m --contimeout 60s \
    --retries 10 --low-level-retries 20 \
    --stats 60s --log-file ~/rclone_scanfi_$year.log --log-level INFO
  echo "=== $(date) Completed $year ==="
done

2c. Verify counts/sizes, then a size-only check against the source:

for year in 1985 1990 1995 2000 2005 2010 2015 2020 2025; do
  rclone size arbutus:predictiveecology/SCANFI_v2/$year
done

rclone check \
  "gdrive:Shared/Hosted/SCANFI/SCANFI v2/1985" \
  "arbutus:predictiveecology/SCANFI_v2/1985" \
  --filter-from ~/scanfi.filter --size-only

The equivalent from R, once buckethost is configured:

bucket_verify("gdrive-or-local/1985", "SCANFI_v2/1985",
              filter_file = "~/scanfi.filter")$ok

Phase 3 — generate the catalogue

From any R session with the arbutus rclone remote configured:

library(buckethost)
options(
  buckethost.endpoint  = "https://object-arbutus.cloud.computecanada.ca",
  buckethost.container = "predictiveecology",
  buckethost.remote    = "arbutus"
)

generate_indexes(
  heading = "PredictiveEcology Temporary Data Repository",
  disclaimer_html = paste0(
    "<div class='hero'>",
    "<p><strong>These data are not produced by the PredictiveEcology group",
    "</strong> and are only hosted here to ease open data access.</p>",
    "<p>For SCANFI v2 data, see the authoritative source: ",
    "<a href='https://open.canada.ca/data/en/dataset/",
    "07653869-f303-46c2-a04e-9ab479b73cbf' target='_blank' rel='noopener'>",
    "open.canada.ca</a></p></div>"
  ),
  host_note = "Hosted on the Digital Research Alliance of Canada's Arbutus object storage."
)

Browse it at https://object-arbutus.cloud.computecanada.ca/predictiveecology/index.html. Re-run generate_indexes() any time data is added or changed.

Phase 4 — the actual payoff: read it from anywhere 

library(terra)
r <- bucket_raster("SCANFI_v2/1985/SCANFI_age_median_1985_v2.tif")
r                                            # metadata only

b   <- SpaDES.tools::randomStudyArea(size = 1e9, seed = 123)
sub <- crop(r, project(vect(b), crs(r)))     # only the AOI's bytes transfer
plot(sub)

Troubleshooting FAQ

  • Every S3 request fails with signature/DNS errors → you forgot force_path_style = true on the rclone S3 remote.
  • openstack ec2 credentials create hangs/refuses → port 5000 is blocked on your network. Run it once from home or a hotspot; the credentials are portable afterwards.
  • OAuth callback never completes on a headless node → the SSH -L 53682:localhost:53682 tunnel isn’t up, or you ran rclone config in a different session than the one with the tunnel.
  • Application Credentials don’t work with rclone → those are Swift-native; you need EC2 credentials for the S3 protocol.
  • Data is in “Shared with me” and rclone can’t see it → add a My Drive shortcut, or use --drive-shared-with-me.

On this page

Developed by Eliot McIntire, PredictiveEcology.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
